name: abnf
documentation: ABNF generates parsers for ABNF grammars. Though intended for use with RFC grammars, ABNF should handle any valid grammar.

modules.name: abnf.typing
documentation:
Conditional typing imports are collected here, because doing the import below in 
parser.py appears to confuse pylance, causing it to flag objects as not conforming to
Parser protocol.  Adding Parser as a superclass of Rule leads to more odd type errors.
The code branches on version instead of using try-except so that mypy is happy.

modules.name: abnf.grammars.rfc2616
documentation:
Collected rules from RFC 2616.
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2616.html

Though RFC 2616 is largely obsolete, some later RFCs, not obsolete, 
still incorporate grammar from RFC 2616; in particular, RFC 6265.
Here we collect grammar from RFC 2616 as needed for use with other RFC grammars.

classes.name: abnf.grammars.rfc2616.Rule
documentation:
Rule objects generated from ABNF in RFC 2616.
Note that token rule is implemented from prose value in RFC 2616, and | was 
replaced by / as expected by RFC 5234.

modules.name: abnf.grammars.misc
documentation: Miscellaneous functions.

functions.name: abnf.grammars.misc.load_grammar_rules
documentation:
A decorator that loads grammar rules following class declaration.  The code assumes
that cls is a Rule subclass with a grammar attribute.
The imported_rules parameter allows one to import rules from other modules. For examples,
see for instance rfc7230.py.

functions.name: abnf.grammars.misc.load_grammar_rulelist
documentation:
A decorator that loads grammar rules following class declaration.  The code assumes
that cls is a Rule subclass with a grammar attribute.
The imported_rules parameter allows one to import rules from other modules. For examples,
see for instance rfc7230.py.

classes.name: abnf.grammars.rfc6265.LocalRule
documentation:
Implementations of some RFC 6265 rules defined as prose values.
The definition of subdomain is taken from RFC1034 and RFC1123, with IPv6 support
added. The definition of subdomain found therein contained left-recursion; the definition
here does not.
The definitions of path-value, extension-av incorporate verified errata.

classes.name: abnf.grammars.rfc6265.Rule
documentation: Rules from RFC 6265.

methods.name: abnf.parser.Parser.lparse
documentation: def parse(
self, source: str, start: int
) -> tuple[Nodes, int]:  # pragma: no cover

classes.name: abnf.parser.Alternation
documentation:
Implements the ABNF alternation operator. -- Alternation(parser1, parser2, ...)
returns a parser that invokes parser1, parser2, ... in turn and returns the result
of the first successful parse..

classes.name: abnf.parser.Concatenation
documentation:
Implements the ABNF concatention operation. Concatention(parser1, parser2, ...)
returns a parser that invokes parser1, parser2, ... in turn and returns a list of Nodes
if every parser succeeds.

classes.name: abnf.parser.Repeat
documentation: Implements the ABNF Repeat operator for Repetition.

classes.name: abnf.parser.Repetition
documentation: Implements the ABNF Repetition operation.

methods.name: abnf.parser.Option.lparse
documentation:
---
param source: source data
param start: offset at which to begin parsing.
raises ParseError: true
returns: parse tree, new offset at which to continue parsing
rtype: Node, int
type str: true
---
Implements the ABNF Option operation.

value is either a string to be matched, or a two-element tuple representing an
inclusive range; e.g. ('a', 'z') matches all letters a-z.

methods.name: abnf.parser.Literal._lparse_range
documentation: Parse source when self.value represents a range.

Represents a terminal literal value.

classes.name: abnf.parser.Rule

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.__new__
documentation: Overrides super().__new__ to implement a symbol table via object caching.

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.exclude_rule
documentation:
Exclude values which match rule.  For example, suppose we have the following
grammar.
foo = %x66.6f.6f
keyword = foo
identifier = ALPHA *(ALPHA / DIGIT )

We don't want to allow a keyword to be an identifier.  To do this,
Rule('identifier').exclude_rule(Rule('keyword'))

Then attempting to use "foo" as an identifier would result in a ParseError.

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.parse
documentation:
---
param source: source data
param start=0: offset at which to begin parsing.
raises GrammarError: |-
  if rule has no definition. This usually means that a
  non-terminal in the grammar is not defined or imported.
raises ParseError: if source cannot be parsed using rule.
returns: parse tree, new offset at which to continue parsing
rtype: Node, int
type str: true
---

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.parse_all
documentation:
Parses the source from beginning to end. If not all of the source is consumed, a
ParseError is raised.

```
param source

source data

type str



param start=0

offset at which to begin parsing.

returns

parse tree

rtype

Node

raises ParseError

if source cannot be parsed using rule.

raises GrammarError

if rule has no definition.  This usually means that a
non-terminal in the grammar is not defined or imported.
```

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.create
documentation:
Creates a Rule object from ABNF source. A terminating CRLF will be appended to
rule_source if needed to satisfy the ABNF grammar rule for "rule".

```
param rule_source

the rule source.

type str



param start=0

the offset at which to begin parsing rule_source.

type int



returns

a Rule object (or subclass of Rule)

raises

ParseError
```

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.load_grammar
documentation:
Loads grammar and attempts to parse it as a rulelist. If successful,
cls is populated with the rules in the rulelist.
When strict = True, line endings following rules are normalized to CRLF to
satisfy the definition of 'rulelist.  If strict is set to False, the grammar
is parsed as is.

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.from_file
documentation:
Loads the contents of path and attempts to parse it as a rulelist. If successful,
cls is populated with the rules in the rulelist.

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.get
documentation:
Retrieves Rule by name.  If a Rule object matching name is found, it is returned.
Otherwise default is returned, and no Rule object is
created, as would be the case when invoking Rule(name).
Note that

methods.name: abnf.parser.Rule.rules
documentation:
Returns a list of all rules created.

:returns: List

A parser generated from an ABNF rule.

To create a Rule object, use Rule.create.

rule = Rule.create('URI = scheme ":" hier-part [ "?" query ] [ "#" fragment ]')

classes.name: abnf.parser.Node

methods.name: abnf.parser.Node.value
documentation: Returns the node value as generated by a parser.
Node objects are used to build parse trees.
### Node classes ####
A parser returns a parse tree of Node objects.  Usually one would then walk the node tree
with a visitor object to do whatever.  A NodeVisitor class, found below, implements
basic reflective visitor.

classes.name: abnf.parser.LiteralNode

methods.name: abnf.parser.LiteralNode.children
documentation: Returns an empty list of children, since LiteralNodes are terminal.
LiteralNode objects are used to build parse trees.

classes.name: abnf.parser.NodeVisitor

documentation: Visit node.  This method invokes the appropriate method for the node type.

methods.name: abnf.parser.NodeVisitor._skip_visit
documentation: Skip node visit.
An external visitor class.
